videos.title: 9 BEST Things to do in Guadalajara, Mexico | Jalisco Top Attractions | Mexico Travel Guide & Tourism
videos.description:
How to find cheap flights and travel more: https://travelforalmostfree.com/ref=beforeyougo2 // Here are the best things to do in Guadalajara, Mexico.

=======================

Fired travel agent wants revenge! Here's the secret to cheap flights: https://bit.ly/3da2cED

=======================

Welcome to Guadalajara, the vibrant capital of the state of Jalisco in Mexico, known for its rich history, colorful architecture, and iconic cultural experiences. In this video, we'll explore some of the top things to do in Guadalajara and its surrounding areas.

One of the most delicious and unique food experiences in Guadalajara is trying Tortas Ahogadas. This traditional dish features a crispy bread roll filled with meat or seafood, drowned in a spicy tomato sauce, and topped with pickled onions. It's a must-try for any foodie visiting Guadalajara.

No trip to Mexico is complete without experiencing the lively energy and music of a Mariachi band. These traditional bands are a beloved part of Mexican culture, and you can catch performances in the streets, restaurants, and bars throughout Guadalajara.

For a taste of Mexico's famous Tequila, head to Visit Tequila, a town just outside of Guadalajara that's known for producing the best tequila in the world. Here, visitors can tour distilleries, sample different varieties of tequila, and learn about the history and production of this iconic Mexican spirit.

Teatro Degollado is a beautiful neoclassical theater located in the heart of Guadalajara's historic downtown area. It's a stunning architectural masterpiece with a rich history, and visitors can catch performances, concerts, and cultural events throughout the year.

Lake Chapala is Mexico's largest lake, and it's a popular destination for boating, fishing, and water sports. The lake is surrounded by charming towns and villages, and visitors can explore the local markets, restaurants, and cultural attractions.

The Guadalajara Zoo is a fantastic family-friendly attraction with over 2,000 animals from around the world, including lions, tigers, giraffes, and elephants. It's a great place to spend a day and learn about different animal species.

For an underwater adventure, head to Acuario Michin, a world-class aquarium that's home to over 2,000 species of marine life. Visitors can see sharks, rays, turtles, and a wide variety of fish, as well as learn about conservation efforts and marine biology.

In conclusion, Guadalajara and its surrounding areas offer a wealth of diverse and exciting experiences, from delicious food to vibrant music and cultural attractions. So come and discover all that Guadalajara and Mexico have to offer!

Keep watching to see the top things to do in the Guadalajara, top attractions in Guadalajara, places to visit in the Guadalajara and Mexico travel guides.

videos.transcript: Guadalajara is home to tons of amazing attractions including its Historical Center a world-class aquarium and one of the biggest lakes in Latin America here are nine things to do in Guadalajara number nine explore the Historical Center how would you like to get a thorough introduction to the terrain and history of Guadalajara well the best way to do this is to explore the Historical Center known locally as Centro historico all you need is an experienced guide by your side and you're ready for an amazing day full of lessons this guide will take you through all the top spots where you'll get first-hand views of the bustling markets fantastic cultural institutions and beautiful cathedrals if you also want to have some quiet time to yourself a stroll through the Historical Center can give you that much needed peace of mind we recommend that you start your exploration at the plaza de armas where you'll see the Guadalajara Cathedral and then take a picture close to Liberation Square meanwhile while there are also several museums around to check out while here number eight Feast on tortes algadas another great way to explore a new culture is to taste local meals of course all that running around various attractions is going to make you hungry and a favorite meal that is highly recommended is Tortas this delicacy is made with chopped fried pork and birote bread then it is drenched in sauce that contains more dried chili peppers this is why its name actually translates to drowned sandwiches you can eat this garnished with Salsas radishes hot sauces and white onion But whichever way you choose best believe that it's so easy to get addicted to this meal due to how delicious it is also if you ever have a hangover while in Guadalajara this local meal is the perfect cure if you want to explore more meals it's a great idea to join a food tour where you'll have the freedom to eat as many dishes as possible while a local guide takes you through the history of each meal number 7. enjoy life mariachi bands Mariachi is possibly the most famous Mexican music in the world the music is so spectacular that it got listed as a part of the UNESCO intangible cultural heritage but then what's so great about this style of music well words can't explain it you'll just have to listen to it yourself to find out how fantastic this music is in Mexico mariachi music is everywhere and you can tour around various spots where you'll get the opportunity to watch live mariachi bands you can also hire a band to serenade you in the evening at the Plaza De Los Mariachis where it's just you and a crowd of true music lovers number six visit tequila to drink tequila no doubt about it tequila is the most famous beverage in Mexico even in movies there's always someone taking a shot of this drink whether it's to drown away pain or to celebrate a major breakthrough but then do you know that this drink shares a name with the town it's from well a few miles away from Guadalajara is the Mexican Town of tequila where there's no shortage of this drink you should surely head over to this town to explore the distilleries and Agave fields and of course enjoy a few shots of tequila you can travel to this town on the famous tequila Express train or on the local bus it depends on how much you're willing to splurge on the trip number five attend an opera at the theater the Teatro de goyado is a neoclassical theater in Guadalajara that has hosted tons of breathtaking performances from Top orchestras and performers from various parts of the world and you can visit to enjoy an opera performance while here you can start by admiring the interior of the building which is filled with Incredible murals and architecture that look out of this world take it a step further by watching any of the regular performances at the theater including symphony orchestras plus if you have something specific in mind you can buy tickets beforehand and possibly be lucky enough to get seats at gala performances during the Mariachi Festival you can also simply stop in at the theater and check out any of the last minute shows available it will definitely be a performance to remember number four take a day trip to Lake Chapala about an hour south of Guadalajara by bus or car is the largest freshwater lake in Mexico this massive Lake known as Lake Chapala is so huge that it has different towns around its Shores where you can visit including ahihik and Chapala this region also has amazing weather that is complemented by its beautiful surroundings and this is why you'll always find lots of people coming around to relax here it's a favorite for retirees and expats too so if you want to check it out pack your bags over the weekend and you'll be there in no time if you're not a fan of crowds then it's best to visit during the week as holidays and weekends are peak days for visits from tourists also there's a boat available here if you would like to take a short trip to the surrounding small Islands by the way if you want to learn how to save money on airfares then click the link in the description below this guide will teach you how to always get the cheapest flights number three visit Guadalajara's Zoo Guadalajara Zoo has been in operation since 1988 and that's a long time for hundreds of species of animals to make it their habitat so you definitely won't be disappointed with your experience here as there are up to 300 species of animals living within the Guadalajara Zoo indeed it's no surprise that this is considered one of the most important zoos in the Latin America region with a huge emphasis on saving animals that are at risk of going extinct surely for animal lovers this is a mini Paradise it's also a great place to visit with friends and family including children plus there are other activities you can engage in within the zoo including a safari where you can feed giraffes and view animals moving around freely however you may have to part with at least a hundred pesos to gain access to the zoo number two drink craft beer next to Tequila another hugely popular Mexican drink is craft beer and it will be lovely to know exactly how this drink tastes luckily there are lots of amazing local breweries in Guadalajara that you can hop in for a quick drink these breweries usually include a few unique ingredients that make one glass of craft beer different from the next so you'll definitely be in for quite a ride a cool Brewery to check out in this city is cerveza minervo where you can take a tour of the facility and taste several beer samples you can also check out the tap rooms of Santa Sabina cerveza Zora and cerveza Loba plus the best part yet is that you won't have to travel as far as they are all located within the same region feel free to decide which one is your favorite number one visit the Aquario Michin acuario Michin is an aquarium that gives you a lovely view of marine life without having to step into the ocean so if you love The Open Seas you'll definitely enjoy the opportunity to admire sea snakes sharks and axolotls in this aquarium moreover as soon as you're done exploring you can head over to the petting zoo for an up close and personal moment with a few adorable animals there's also a food court to unwind with a hearty meal after a long day of communing with nature if you're feeling adventurous you can pay a small fee for a close encounter with the sharks rest assured that you will have stories to tell for days click here for more things to do in Mexico

videos.title: Top Things to Do in Guadalajara, Mexico | Ultimate Jalisco Travel Guide 2025
videos.description:
Welcome to Guadalajara — the vibrant heart of Jalisco, Mexico!
Famous for its rich history, stunning colonial architecture, and world-renowned culture, Guadalajara offers an unforgettable travel experience. In this Guadalajara Travel Guide we’ll show you the best things to do in Guadalajara, including top attractions, hidden gems, and must-see spots in the surrounding area.

We’ll also offer practical advice to make your visit as smooth as possible, like the best way to buy your tickets and how to travel to the city. 

Whether you’re a first-timer or a seasoned visitor, our guide is designed to enrich your days in Guadalajara. Let’s embark on this amazing adventure together!

▬▬▬▬▬▬ T I M E S T A M P S   ▬▬▬▬▬▬
0:00​​ - Intro
0:18 - How to Travel to the city
0:34 - Historic Center
0:43 - Guadalajara Cathedral
0:58 - Rotonda de los Jaliscienses
1:05 - Teatro Degollado
1:28 - Hospicio Cabañas
2:08 - Tlaquepaque
2:53 - Mercado San Juan de Dios
3:25 - Tequila
3:53 - Lake Chapala
4:39 - Barranca de Huentitán
5:16 - Zapopan Basilica
5:44 - Templo Expiatorio del Santísimo Sacramento
6:11 - Bosque Los Colomos
7:07 - Avenida Chapultepec
7:30 - Museo de la Ciudad de Guadalajara
7:55 - The best time to visit Guadalajara
8:08 - Outro

Visit Tequila:
https://www.tripadvisor.com/Attractions-g775272-Activities-c42-Tequila.html

Subscribe:
https://www.youtube.com/martijnaroundtheworld?sub_confirmation=1

Social Media:
Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/martijnkolkman/

Keywords:
#guadalajara #guadalajaramexico #guadalajarajalisco
tags: top things to do in guadalajara
videos.transcript: Hi, I am Martijn and welcome to Mexico. This is Guadalajara. I spent 5 days in Mexico's second city, gathering the best things you can do and see. From the stunning cathedral and the largest lake in Mexico... ...to the old town, nature and everything in between. The city is served by its own international airport and to travel to the city you can use a taxi, Uber or bus. The journey takes around 30 to 60 minutes depending on the traffic. And you can buy your taxi ticket in the arrivals hall just before the exit. Let's start in the heart of Guadalajara, the historic center, where it all began. It is centered around four main squares forming a cross around the Guadalajara Cathedral. The cathedral itself combines Gothic, Baroque, and Neo-classical styles and dates back to the 16th century. The towers were constructed in the 19th century after the originals were destroyed in an earthquake. Around it you will find Rotonda de los Jaliscienses Ilustres honoring the state's heroes. And the Teatro Degollado, an exquisite neo-classical building. You can take a look inside every Tuesday and Friday between 1 and 3 totally free of charge. Street performers, fountains and vendors fill the squares with life. This area is ideal for walking, taking pictures and soaking in the atmosphere of old Guadalajara. One of the most famous buildings in the historic center is the Hospicio Cabañas. It's full with arts and history. Originally a shelter for orphans, but nowadays it's a cultural center and a UNESCO World Heritage site. It's famous for the murals by José Clemente Orozc, one of Mexico's greatest muralists. His masterpiece, The Man of Fire, covers the chapel's dome and symbolizes revolution and transformation. The museum also hosts art exhibitions and workshops, making it a hub for contemporary art lovers. In a moment, you know more about the place where Tequila is made and the largest lake in Mexico. A colorful and creative place to visit is Tlaquepaque. It's full of local charm. You can shop and eat over there and enjoy the live music... ...and it's only a 30 minutes taxi drive from the city center of Guadalajara. It is known for its vibrant artisan community. The area is lined with colonial style buildings, housing galleries, handmade pottery shops and family-run restaurants. El Parián, the largest cantina in Mexico, is a must visit spot for live mariachi music. The district is ideal for picking up handcrafted souvenirs and relaxing with traditional Mexican cuisine. The biggest indoor market of Latin America is located here in Guadalajara. Mercado San Juan de Dios, also known as Mercado Libertad. also known as Mercado Libertad. Opened in 1958 and designed by Austrian-born Mexican architect Alejandro Zohn. This massive market spends over more than 40,000 square meters across three levels... ...making it not only an architectural landmark, but also a cultural icon. This is the place where locals buy everything from cowboy boots to electronics. Nearby Guadalajara, Tequila is located. And yes, this is the place where the famous drink is made, also known as the killer. It's a 1 hour and 30 minutes drive to Tequila from Guadalajara. And there are several tours. You can visit some distilleries. There are tasting tours and you can see the making process of Tequila. My advice is to book your tickets in advance and you can use the link in the text under this video. A relaxing and beautiful spot is located a 1-hour drive from Guadalajara, Chapala. Lake Chapala is the largest freshwater lake in Mexico and a peaceful getaway from the city. It's a beloved retreat for those seeking peace and natural beauty. The lake's calm waters and gentle breezes create a chill atmosphere. Chapala's charming waterfront promenade is ideal for strolls. And this town is filled with colorful flowers and historic architecture. And you can also take a short boat ride to a nearby island. One of the most beautiful natural wonders in the city is an hidden canyon, Barranca de Huentitán. It's wild, it's beautiful, perfect for hiking or just a great spot to sit down and relax. It is one of Guadalajara's most overlooked natural wonders. The canyon plunges over 500 m deep and stretches for miles... ...forming a dramatic natural border on the northeastern edge of the city. It's part of a protected natural area and serves as a green lung for the city. Up next, more breathtaking nature and a glimpse into one of the most vibrant streets in the city. The city is full with cathedrals and churches and the Zapopan Basilica is one of them. The official name is The Basílica de Nuestra Señora de Zapopan and it dates back to the 17th century. Every October over a million people take place in La Romería. It's a religious pilgrimage bringing a statue from the Guadalajara Cathedral back to Zapopan. And Zapopan... It's a known city but part of the greater metropolitan of Guadalajara. Another beautiful church is the Templo Expiatorio del Santísimo Sacramento. It's a Neo-classic masterpiece famous for its stained glass windows and crafted stonework. It was built in the 20th century using old-world techniques. So no modern tools. And each part of the facade tells a different story from the Bible. If you want to escape the hustle and bustle in the city, then head to Bosque Los Colomos . It's a lush urban forest located in the northwestern part of Guadalajara. Covering about 92 hectares, this park is a great place for walking, jogging, horseback riding, or relaxing in the picnic areas. One of the highlights is the peaceful Japanese garden. That symbolized the friendship between Guadalajara and Kyoto in Japan. The park also has historic importance. In the late 19th century, its natural springs were used to supply drinking water to the city. This famous building called El Castillo, built in 1902, was the center of this water system. Today, it is a cultural center where you can take art or dance classes or enjoy cultural events. One of the trendiest and most vibrant streets in the city is Avenida Chapultepec. It's lined with trees, bars, restaurants, and boutiques. This is the place where locals gather day and night. In the evening, it comes alive with live music and also street performers. And on the weekends, there are several markets. Located in a charming 18th century convent is the City Museum of Guadalajara offering a glimpse into the city's past. The museum showcases the city's evolution from pre-Hispanic times to the modern era. Through permanent exhibits and rotating displays. It's a great starting point to understand the culture, history, and art of this dynamic city... ...and it is free to visit. What is the best time for our visit? I think between October and March. A lot of outdoor activities, events, and really nice weather. If you are not a fan of rain and heat, skip the wet season. And that's between June and September. Please give a thumbs up! Thank you so much. When you like this Guadalajara travel guide. And if you don't want to miss new episodes, just subscribe on my channel. It's totally free and it enriches your view on the whole world.

safetyNotes: And if you don't want to miss new episodes, just subscribe on my channel

videos.title: Exploring Guadalajara Like a Local: Art, Food, and Culture Guide (2024)
videos.description:
Exploring Guadalajara Like a Local: Art, Food, and Culture Guide (2024)

Welcome to Guadalajara, the vibrant capital of Jalisco, where mariachi, tequila, and rich culture come to life. Today, I’m taking you on a journey through this incredible city that’s close to my heart. With the help of my family, I’ve captured footage that showcases not just the touristy spots, but also the hidden gems that make Guadalajara unique.

In this video, you’ll find a mix of everything—delicious street food, stunning art, historic architecture, and lively day trips. Whether you’re planning your first trip to Guadalajara or looking to experience it like a local, this guide is for you. We’ll visit the iconic Cathedral of Guadalajara, marvel at the artistic talents displayed in Plaza de Armas, and soak in the vibrant atmosphere of Plaza Liberación.

I’ll also share personal stories and experiences that make this trip special, from attending Mass at the stunning Templo Expiatorio to wandering the colorful streets of Guadalajara. Along the way, you’ll see why Guadalajara is a city full of life, where the people are warm, the culture is rich, and there’s always something happening—even when the power goes out.

This isn’t just a travel video; it’s a deep dive into the soul of Guadalajara. So join me as we explore the city’s art, food, and culture together. And if you have any questions or need tips for your own trip, don’t hesitate to drop a comment below. Let’s discover Guadalajara’s vibrant and modern spirit together!

our next stop was this cactus garden I'm not overly interested in cactus gardens but my cousin wanted to take me and I'm pretty down for whatever so here we are it was cool and I don't think we paid [Music] [Music] another dayos I went to San Juan goala it's a bit pricey I think around 400 or 22 USD my aunt paid for me this place can get super crowded so avoid weekends and holidays this place is the lazy person in you I never do stuff like this traveling for me is a great ordeal I'm into climbing mountains scuba diving getting into situations I never travel for this but it was super welcoming it was literally a day of natural hot spring saunas eating and relaxing so to slide uphill it's not too bad it's pretty hot though I have to thank Asia for making hot weather tolerable because it's not too bad but I do everything cheap like I'm not going to pay for a car to take me up I'll just walk always try to save as much as I can but I can see people being pretty tired my cousins I long ago passed them up so uh I can see most people probably wanting to take a car or bus up here but it's not too bad and we're here before hitting tequila we stopped by guachi Monon in terms of Pimpin guachi Monon isn't as cool as many ruins you will see in southern Mexico but nonetheless still impressive Los guachi Monon is one of many the chlan culture sites within the tequila Valley sorry uh I will mispronounce some words these ruins which date back over 2,000 years are known for their conical-shaped pyramids which is a unique feature in comparison to many other Pyramids in Mexico this whole area along with the Gila is a UNESCO heritage site worth checking out in conjunction with tequila it has a small Museum and a view of laa Lake if you haven't eaten go to the restaurant SOI chlan after you visit guachi Monon for some bomb ciche and a nice view of laa Lake finally we arrive in Tequila we made a lot of stops but I'd say well under 2 hours from guara I've been here before and did some streets there's still a lot of things happening and some action going on so it's no loss if you don't do it [Music] [Music] next we hit BOS De La Prima I went here with my cousin it's about 1 hour from guara you have to go through a village so we stop by Don Esters for some tacos anything with a Don or daa usually gets my attention because I know it's OG in the forest you can camp here and it's quite beautiful with its pine trees but its main feature is its river which is pretty hot it can get very hot depending where you go so watch out similar to San Juan Gala but far cheaper and real if you want a more natural type location for thermal baths go here you can find pools that are deeper to relax and waterfalls that pound your head feet and back for that nice massage and while you you don't have to walk in the river we chose to and I didn't have shoes for it it's pretty brutal on your feet so make sure you bring some sandals or waterproof shoes um or just take the paths that works as [Music] well well there's a quick overview of not only what Alara but how I like to travel I make enough money to travel but not enough to eat out fat go to tons of tourist attractions and pay for this and that oh all my trips are minimal and cost because what's exciting to me is engaging with people eating locally and wandering the streets and of course hitting any free events this how I travel while it does help to speak the language I do this in any country I go to but as always you got to have some Street smarts because many things can happen while traveling I never really had any bad experiences in Mexico and I've probably been over 50 times I hope this video is inspired you to want to come to guara or Mexico in general stay tuned for more videos peace and keep being curious

videos.title: 5 Months as a Gringo in Guadalajara and Why Mexico Rocks
videos.description:
Thinking about moving to Mexico or curious what life is really like in a major Mexican city as a U.S. citizen? After spending 5 months living in Guadalajara, Mexico’s 3rd-largest city, I’m sharing an honest review of food, healthcare, transportation, safety, digital nomad life, and expat living based entirely on my personal experience.

In this video, I cover:
00:00 – Intro & who this channel is for
00:30 – My story: moving to Guadalajara to job hunt & build this channel
00:58 – Food in Guadalajara: street tacos, global cuisine, restaurants, and more
02:00 – Healthcare in Mexico: public vs. private, pharmacies, Red Cross urgent care, and medical concierge services
09:00 – Transportation in Guadalajara: buses, metro light rail, Uber
11:23 – Why I’m leaving Mexico and traveling next to Belize & Guatemala
12:50 – Expat vs. immigrant — what’s the difference?
13:20 – Spanish language level needed for Mexico solo travel and cultural awareness
14:10 – Safety tips for tourists & expats
15:50 – Final thoughts: Why Mexico is amazing

Who this channel helps
The S.P.E.N.D. Channel supports empty-nest single parents ages 40–60 in the U.S. who want to relocate abroad, explore life as an expat, and build a lifestyle-based future through travel, flexibility, and courage.

Topics in this video
— Living in Guadalajara as a U.S. citizen
— Mexico travel tips & real costs
— Healthcare in Mexico for tourists & expats
— Using pharmacies & Red Cross urgent care (Cruz Roja Mexicana)
— Public transportation in Mexico (bus & light rail)
— Safety, phone theft awareness & defensive walking
— English-teaching job search in Mexico
— Traveling onward to Belize & Guatemala
— Spanish learning for expats (recommended B1 level)

My Medical Concierge (Lori Payne)
https://www.facebook.com/groups/401836104277179

Berkshire Hathaway Travel Protection
https://www.bhtp.com/

WorldTrips Travel Insurance
https://worldtrips.com

Next steps
Subscribe for weekly videos on relocating abroad, travel logistics, cost of living, language learning, and digital nomad life over 40.

👉 Subscribe here: https://youtube.com/@thespendchannel
👉 Free relocation checklist: https://thespendchannel.kit.com/33e6ab5b09
👉 15-minute FREE fit call to discuss your situation: https://thespendchannel.com/contact
👉 Support the channel: https://buymeacoffee.com/thespendchannel

Tags
#Guadalajara #MexicoTravel #LivingInMexico #MoveAbroad #DigitalNomad #ExpatLife #SingleParents #RelocateAbroad #MexicoHealthcare #GuadalajaraMexico #EmptyNester #LifeOver40
videos.transcript: Are you a US citizen who's curious about what life is like in a major Mexican city? Then stay tuned, because in this video, I'm gonna go over the food, healthcare, and transportation here in Guadalajara, Mexico's third largest city. If you're new here, my name is Jason. This is the Spend Channel, S-P-E-N-D. That's single parent, empty nester, digital nomad, where I help empty nest single parents ages 40 to 60 in the United States put together a game plan for relocating abroad. So let me tell you my story. I arrived here Guadalajara, May 25th, with a simple goal. Actually two goals. Work on this YouTube channel and look for an English teaching job. I'm TEFL certified. Came here with my retirement savings. I started looking for job online and then offline at language schools here locally. And that didn't work out. I wasn't able to secure a position. If you wanna know the details about that, I have that in another video. But here, first thing I'm gonna do is talk about the food. If you're a foodie, Guadalajara is for you. There's so many different types of cuisines available. Obviously you have some great Mexican food options, street tacos, my opinion, no trip to Mexico is complete without a visit to a street taco stand and they're fantastic here. Also, this is the state of Jalisco where tequila was invented. Also, mariachi bands. You have so many different types of options. There's besides Mexican food, you've got your traditional American staples like hamburgers and hot dogs. That's easy. Lots of pizza options available. I had Colombian food for lunch one time. I found a Uruguayan restaurant on Google Maps. Haven't gone there, but Uruguay, even Uruguayan food is available. There's sushi. There's Chinese food, steakhouses, which that's kind of on the expensive side. Shoot, I think that covers everything. Just, there's no shortage of food options here. You can go to a different, if you have the means, you can go to a different restaurant every day and not get bored. And so yes, well done Mexico on the food. Next topic is healthcare. There's the, you got a public health system and you got a private healthcare system. First, I'll talk about the public option. If you're not a resident or a citizen, then you're not eligible for the public system. That's IMSS and IMSABI. I have another video for healthcare, but if you want to come here as a tourist like I am, you want to come with travel insurance. I have a travel medical insurance policy through Berkshire Hathaway that covers major medical, like heart attacks, strokes, major accidents, that kind of thing. Everything else I pay for in cash. My first line of defense are the local pharmacies. If you have like a skin rash, insect bite, stomach trouble, you go to a local pharmacy, like Farmacia Guadalajara, Farmacias Benavides, Farmacias Similares. You go there and without an appointment, you see a doctor right then and there on the spot. You pay them 70, 80, 90 pesos whatever it is, and they'll, you know, take your complaint and they'll write down their recommendation, what drugs you need to buy, and then you pay the doctor there, and then you go literally into the pharmacy. And you give them the prescription or the list, whatever it is, and they will fill it for you right there. And it's paid for in cash. You could either pay in efectivo, which is physical paper cash, or you could use your debit or credit card, either or. And I've used that system several times and it's been very transparent and efficient in and out and under an hour. Very, very user friendly. I'm very happy with it. So that's my first line of defense. The next thing up from there is the La Cruz Roja Mexicana. That's the Mexican Red Cross. I used that system once in July of last year when I was a student here. It's like urgent care. And then you go there, ideally you want to get to La Cruz Roja early in the morning. That's when the lines are shortest. And you definitely need to know some Spanish because there are barely any English speakers there. Maybe one or two. Bring Google Translate if you're not very good with Spanish and um, you pay an entrance fee to get in and then they'll take your complaint. I was, I thought I had had, Montezuma's revenge. I drank orange juice from a street vendor in Tlaquepaque a couple of days prior to that. So. I thought maybe that's what my problem was. So I was put in a bed, they put in an IV line and administered some IV drugs. I paid for those with my debit card. It was a few hundred pesos. And then after about three or four hours, they gave me a diagnosis. They said, you don't have montezuma's revenge. What you have is a fungal infection in your leg. Which made sense because I had had a sore on one of the, one of my toes on my right foot, and that apparently let fungus in, and it went all the way up my leg towards my groin. It was pretty nasty. My whole right leg was red and inflamed. I was limping when I was walking. It was a really bad problem. So I'm glad I saw them when I did. And so they, the diagnosis, they gave me some, some physical paper prescriptions, and then they checked me out. I had to pay an exit fee, and then I went to Farmacia Guadalajara or Farmacias Benavides, whichever local pharmacy it was, I don't remember specifically. Got my prescriptions filled. All told, the entire day, my medical care was 600 pesos. That's, I don't know, $35, $40 USD, give or take, depends on exchange rates, but that was for an entire day of medical care. Very efficient, very transparent about the price. This is what you want, this is what you will pay. No copay bullshit. No deductible bullshit. No in and out of network bullshit. No pre-certification drama, none of that harassment and bureaucracy in the US. Now, there is a medical healthcare bureaucracy in here in Mexico, of course, but it's so much easier and user-friendly here in my experience, from my experience as a gringo tourist. I'm very happy with the medical care system, and that's just the public sector. For private healthcare, I have a medical concierge. Her name is Lori Payne. I'll put her description and her information into the description of this video, but she runs my medical concierge. She has a Facebook group. She's also available on WhatsApp, and that's another great thing about healthcare doctors. They all use, a lot of people here use WhatsApp. It's gospel here. Gotta use WhatsApp. So you send her a message on either Facebook Messenger or WhatsApp, and she will arrange for you to get an appointment. I needed a gastroenterologist for a problem I had had, and so she hooked me up with a local gastroenterologist. He was, he's in Zapopan, which that's a city to the west of Guadalajara. It's a pretty big city, and so I went to my appointment with my doctor in Zapopan, and Lori joined me. It was like having a friend, a trusted friend with you or a family member. She asked him questions I didn't think of asking, and his English was perfectly good. The entire appointment was a full hour from just getting to know me in my medical history. I used to have ulcerative colitis about over 20 years ago, and I had major surgery to correct it, to cure it, so he had to hear my history and it was very unrushed, very casual. He had me on his table, did a, like a physical exam of my stomach area, and he told me I needed follow up care. And so through my medical concierge, he was able to get me an ultrasound appointment, which I attended a few weeks down the road. Oh, with the gastroenterologist, I paid when it was over. It was, uh, a thousand pesos, 900 or 1900 pesos. I forgot the price, but it was transparent. This is the service you got, a consult with a gastro specialist. This is the price you will pay. How do you want to pay, efectivo or card? And I pay with my tarjeta. That means card. And so that was done. And then Lori got to work on getting me an ultrasound appointment, and that was at a lab. And that lab was within walking distance of my hostel room. I'm currently, I've been living in Colonia Americana, Lafayette area, and my, the lab I went to was on Avenida Mexico, so it was maybe a 15, 20 minute walk. I went in, I told them in Spanish what I was there for. I had like, I had like the information about the appointment that was sent to me over email in Spanish, of course, which again, use DeepL or Google Translate if you have something in Spanish that you don't understand, it's easy. And so I went in, I checked in at the front desk. I think I paid either before or after the appointment was over. It was an ultrasound. It was expected to take over an hour. They were done in like 20, 30 minutes. And when it was over, I paid with my card, the payment amount, they said it was gonna be. No, again, no bullshit, totally transparent. And that was over. And then they sent the results of my ultrasound to my medical concierge and the doctor. And then I think I had another follow-up appointment with him down the road. Again, same, easy, convenient, transparent, user-friendly experience. So if you have the means, use the private system. If you need specialized care, use the private system. But for ordinary common problems, bug bites, skin problems, stomach trouble, the flu, use the public healthcare system first. That's my honest advice. And so that's healthcare. Now I want to talk about transportation. Mexico, in my opinion, based upon personal experience, does public transportation much better than in the United States. I'll talk about the bus. The bus system here, it's complicated and it's easy to take the wrong bus, but once you take the right bus, you'll get to where you're going pretty quickly and efficiently. It's 9.5 pesos per trip. What is that? 55, 60 cents USD? Something like that. You could pay either with coins, which are monedas, pay with coins, or you could buy a plastic card and then use, and you just scan that when you get on the bus. And Google Maps is very helpful for getting you where you need to be. One thing about Google Maps, it's not 100% accurate, so it does make mistakes, but it's overall pretty reliable. And of course, you could always ask a local in Spanish, "es este la linea tres"? Line three is what I use to get to central part of Guadalajara from here, Colonia Americana. What I really like though is the light rail, the metro light rail system. It's fantastic. Same price, 9.5 pesos. You can get from one end of Guadalajara to the other. You can go from the north end to the south end of the city, or go from northeast to south or northwest to southeast, or vice versa, in like a half an hour. It's crazy fast. Sometimes it's above ground, sometimes it's underground, but it's really quick and efficient and it's often very crowded because it's very popular. You've got commuters going to and from work, ordinary people running errands, but yeah, I can't say enough positive things about the Metro Light Rail system here. It's fantastic. The bus system? My only complaint is that it's confusing. It's easy to take the wrong bus. I've done it before. You'll survive. If it makes an unexpected turn, just get off and figure out where to go from there. It's, you'll be okay. Promise you that. So that's the public transportation system. Uber here is plentiful. Most of my trips around town have been all been less than $10. It's pretty efficient, although traffic could be pretty nasty, especially during rush hour. What do you expect from a city of 8 million people? When you take the city of Guadalajara plus Zapopan, plus Tlaquepaque and Tonala, Tlajomulco De Zuniga, Zuniga, that's a city south of here. When you factor in Guadalajara and all its suburbs, you're looking at about seven, eight million people. So it's a big, very big city. Public transportation runs really well for a city this size. So that's that. So I talked about food, healthcare, transportation. I give them all five stars. Well done. Okay. I wasn't able to qualify for residency, so I did what I could and came here as a tourist with the goal of getting an English teaching job, which didn't work out. And so I need to leave the country. In fact, I'm leaving tomorrow. I'm gonna be flying from here, Guadalajara to Cancun. I'll spend a night at hotel there, and then I'm taking the Tren Maya from, from Cancun to Chetumal. That's in the southern portion of Quintana Roo. That's the Eastern most state in the country. And then on the morning of November 1st, I'm gonna walk into Belize on foot, cross into Belize on foot, and I will be in Belize for four weeks for the first time in 10 years. I visited Belize in 2015. Had a great time there. Always wanted to come back. And so yeah, I'll be in Belize for four weeks while waiting to hear from the US government. I applied for a job with the US Patent Office, and so I'm expecting an offer to come down in mid-November, so I'll be in Belize when that happens. And then at the end of November, I'm gonna be taking a bus into Guatemala. I've never been to Guatemala before. I've got hostel reservations in Antigua and there I'll be staying until I return to the US if I get the job offer. If not, I'll remain in Guatemala up to 90 days, which I'll be authorized to do. And if I'm gonna be in Guatemala that length of time, I'm gonna be looking at volunteer work exchange opportunities through worldpackers.com or workaway.info, one of those two to keep my cost of living down and find something rewarding to do like volunteer work. I think I've covered everything I wanted to cover, so my advice to those US citizen gringos who wanna move here either on a permanent basis, which those are immigrants. If you're cutting ties with your former country with the intent to establish a new life in a new country, you're an immigrant. But if you're like me, you're only here part-time and you're gonna be returning to your home country or moving onward to a different country, then in my book, you're an expat because your presence is temporary. You're gonna be moving on or moving back, so that I wanted to settle that debate. Anything else? Oh yeah. So in terms of advice for expats or immigrants, obviously you wanna learn the language as best you can. If you're gonna be traveling by yourself extensively in Mexico, you want to get to a B1 level. In Espanol, that's lower level intermediate. You'll be proficient enough to handle most situations that come up when you travel. You could handle a basic conversation provided that the other party paces themselves and you might need them to repeat themselves and you'll be, you're working with a limited vocabulary, but it's good enough. You'll make a lot of mistakes, but they'll be able to understand you. You'll be able to get things done. And so that's where, you know, at B1, that's where the fun happens. If you're A1 or A2, it might be a bit too much for you and you'll, you would need another person to help you out. It's based on personal experience. Uh, but yeah, learn the language. Respect the culture. Culture refers to customs, traditions, routines, the way of life, the manana culture. Oh yeah. I wanted to talk about safety concerns. My biggest safety concern is crossing the street. Traffic here is kind of chaotic. My best advice to you is, you know, in the US we have defensive driving. Well here, practice defensive walking, wait for traffic to clear before you cross the street, even if you have a green light. Yeah, you do have the right of way in a crosswalk. Yeah. But people here don't wanna stop if they don't have to. They'll like squeak around you, go behind you, in front of you. It's just how it is. You're not gonna change that. But be very careful. Look both ways, even if it's a one way street, because somebody could be coming the wrong way on a bike or a scooter, and it can hit you if you're not looking out. Also, this is a big thing. Cell phone theft is a thing here in Guadalajara. I've heard stories of people on motorcycles or mopeds, just yanking cell phones straight outta people's hands when they're on a sidewalk. So don't be that person who's walking and talking because you're a sitting duck. Also, the sidewalks have a lot of problems like uproot. You know, the tree roots will uproot a sidewalk, will cause a sidewalk to pop up, and so that's a tripping hazard. A lot of sidewalks have weird holes in them. You could get your ankle caught in one and trip and fall, potentially break an ankle. In which case you'll be using the public healthcare system. But still that's not a way you want to do it. So practice situational awareness at all times when you're walking, especially when you're walking, crossing the street. If you're on a sidewalk, be extra diligent. Maybe give yourself a few extra minutes to get where you're going. Use Google Maps, but don't be glued to your cell phone either. You gotta watch where you're going. That's very important, you know, for the aforementioned reasons of either cell phone theft or just tripping and falling. So yeah, Mexico, wonderful country. I've been here quite a bit. I've visited five different states. Sonora, Sinaloa, Baja California, Baja California Sur, Jalisco, Mexico City. And on my way to Belize, I'll be passing through Quintana Roo. So that's, I don't know, seven states. I've seen quite a bit of this country. But to be totally honest with you, my knowledge of this amazing country just scratches the surface. It's a cultural powerhouse. The food is fantastic. The people are very friendly and chill, except when they're driving. They just want to get where they're going. Traffic is designed for efficiency and flow, so if you're stopping both ends of traffic to cross the street in a crosswalk, that's a disruption to the flow of traffic. That's probably how the locals see it, although I don't wanna speak for anyone here. I'm not a local, I'm not a citizen, i'm not even a resident, just a humble tourist. But I've honestly enjoyed my time here. I would not have been here five months if I didn't like it. Oh, there's so much to offer. My Spanish continues to improve all the time. And, I think with that I will wrap up this video. So thank you for watching. Again, my name is Jason, the Spend Channel, single parent empty nester digital nomad, where I help empty nest single parents ages 40 to 60 in the US build a game plan for relocating abroad. And that's that, I'm signing off. And any information in this video, I'll have links in the description below. Thanks again for watching. Take care. We'll see you in the next one.
